content: 5 min read Just now -- Press enter or click to view image in full size liquid glass on the webA couple of days ago, I came across a post on Hacker News titled "Real liquid glass for the web" — my first thought was: here we go again? After WWDC, projects that "recreate Apple's effects" have been popping up like mushrooms after rain, and nine out of ten just use `backdrop-filter` to slap on a frosted glass look and call it a day. I clicked in and was stunned — this thing actually bends the web page. It's not a blur, not a screenshot. When you move your mouse over it, the text and images truly deform beneath the glass, and you can still select text and click links. One developer, 4 days, 311 stars. The project is called liquid-glass. How does it "bend" the web? Other "liquid glass" libraries on the market generally take two routes: The first uses `backdrop-filter: url()`, which only works in Chrome — in Safari and Firefox it degrades to a simple blur. Not glass, just a nearsighted effect. The second uses WebGL to first screenshot the page (via `html2canvas`), then applies distortion on the image — it looks like glass, but you can't select the text underneath, can't click links, and the content is "dead". Plus it drags in three.js, pushing the bundle size toward hundreds of KB. liquid-glass takes a smarter approach: SVG displacement filter. Let me explain the principle simply: first, generate a signed distance field (SDF) for a rounded rectangle, and encode the distance information into a displacement map — the red channel represents horizontal offset, the green channel vertical offset, and the blue channel is a specular highlight mask. This map is then fed into SVG's `feDisplacementMap` filter, followed by a three-channel RGB split to create chromatic aberration. Because the filter acts directly on the real DOM, the underlying text remains selectable and links clickable. And it doesn't take screenshots, doesn't use canvas, doesn't use WebGL — zero runtime dependencies, with the only peer dependency being React. Three modes, more interesting the more you look The author provides a `` component with three modes covering almost all scenarios: ① In-place mode — give Glass a size and center, and it bends its own children. Great for cards, hero areas. 60fps animation follows the mouse, and the React component doesn't need to re-render. ② Refract mode — float a glass lens over any content. For example, a notification card hovering over a photo, where the card is glass material and you can see the distorted photo through it. Use `refract={}` to specify the bending target, and the `behind` property to fill edge bleeding. ③ src/draw mode — for video and canvas. SVG filters can't handle dynamic media, so this takes a GPU path: a renderer samples video frames and draws multiple independent glass lenses on top. For instance, each control button in a video player can be an independent piece of liquid glass. Press enter or click to view image in full size Set up in just two lines Install the package: npm i @samasante/liquid-glassTurn a button into glass: import { Glass } from "@samasante/liquid-glass"; Save No CSS import, no provider configuration, no presets needed. Whatever color you give it becomes the hue, and children render clearly on top — crisp, not blurry. Works seamlessly with Tailwind — just use className and style, all sizes, roundness, and colors are fully controllable. Optical parameters until you're satisfied What impressed me most about the author's attention to detail is the set of optics parameters — instead of choosing presets, every optical property is broken down for you to tweak: • strength — refractive strength, maximum pixel offset (0 to 1) • depth — how far the bending extends from the edge toward the center • curvature — convex curvature, the "liquid magnification" in the middle • dispersion — chromatic aberration intensity, RGB separation at edges • bend — edge refraction, the thickness of that glass "lip" • frost — frosted level • sheen / sheenAngle / sheenWidth — position and concentration of directional highlights These parameters drive both DOM mode and WebGL mode — one vocabulary, two rendering paths. The author also provides default values so you can use it without tweaking — but once you start, you won't stop. Why I think this project deserves attention Not because of the star count — 311 stars isn't much on today's GitHub. What moved me is that the author solved a problem that "everyone said was impossible": Create a true liquid glass effect on the web while keeping DOM interactivity. On the market, either it "looks like glass but is a dead screenshot," or it "only works in Chrome." liquid-glass is the first to achieve all three: real bending + interactive + cross-browser. And I particularly admire the author's handling of Safari. WebKit's filter implementation has numerous pitfalls — `feDisplacementMap` behaves completely differently in Safari, causing many libraries to give up. liquid-glass includes WebKit-specific fixes: single-precision filter resolution, shape bitmap reconstruction, filter id cache refresh — these details are documented in `BROWSERS.md`. After reading it, you'll feel the author isn't showing off, but genuinely wants to bring this effect to everyone. Summary liquid-glass is a project that simply "got it right." It's not an AI wrapper, nor another bandwagon "doing XX with LLM." It's just a frontend engineer seeing Apple's Liquid Glass effect and thinking: Can I make it real on the web? The answer is: yes. And it's simpler than you think — one `` component, zero dependencies, MIT license, ready to use. Project homepage: github.com/samasante/liquid-glass Live demo: glass.samasante.com Stars: 311 (4 days, as of 2026-06-26) License: MIT What's the most "wow" frontend effect you've ever seen? Let's chat in the comments.
